ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated oxtail code; A0CKL (cattle meat raw) is the closest defensible parent node. Tail-cut specificity is noted in the description.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for raw oxtail; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw oxtail serving is approximately 150 g, representing one to two bone-in tail segments before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Oxtail, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values for energy, protein, fat, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol taken directly from HPB Foodo record for Oxtail, raw (cr_id F06030041). Carbohydrate is reported as not available (-1) in the source. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 13325 (beef oxtail, raw) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions.

gi_source.title: N/A — Oxtail, raw contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic index is not applicable
gi_source.source_match_notes: Oxtail raw contains negligible carbohydrates (carbohydrate_g reported as not available by HPB; effectively 0 g net carbohydrate for a pure meat cut). Glycemic index is not applicable for this food. GL per serving is 0.0 by definition. glycemic_index is set to the numeric sentinel value 0 to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric; gi_category is set to not_applicable to signal that GI testing is not meaningful for this food.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high saturated fat content (~19.8 g/100 g) contributing to arterial stiffness and cardiovascular risk; sodium is moderate at 50 mg/100 g in the raw state
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit raw oxtail to occasional small portions (≤100 g cooked), trim all visible fat before cooking, and avoid high-sodium braising sauces such as soy sauce or oyster sauce that would substantially increase the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high total fat (47.1 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~19.8 g/100 g) impair insulin sensitivity and worsen insulin resistance, despite negligible carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption of raw oxtail due to its very high saturated fat content, which impairs insulin signalling; if consumed, portions should be limited to 50–70 g cooked on rare occasions with all visible fat trimmed and no added sugar in preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (~19.8 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.5 g/100 g from ruminant sources) significantly elevate LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk; cholesterol content is 76 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat raw oxtail as an occasional food only, strictly trim all external fat before cooking, avoid high-fat braising methods, and keep portions to ≤80 g cooked to minimise saturated fat and trans fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High arachidonic acid from beef amplifies inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); saturated fat (~19.8 g/100 g) and ruminant trans fat (~0.5 g/100 g) are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit raw oxtail, as its high saturated fat, ruminant trans fat, and arachidonic acid content collectively amplify joint inflammation; if consumed, trim all visible fat and limit to very small portions infrequently.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oxtail, raw, refers to the uncooked tail portion of beef cattle, sold bone-in with surrounding muscle, cartilage, and a substantial layer of gelatinous connective tissue and fat. The raw meat displays a deep red colour with visible marbling and thick fat deposits. Oxtail is prized in many cuisines — including Eurasian, Caribbean, Korean (kkori gomtang), South African (oxtail potjie), and British — for its rich, deeply savoury flavour when slow-cooked or braised, as the collagen in the connective tissue converts to gelatin, producing a thick, unctuous sauce. Nutritionally, raw oxtail is very high in total fat (47.1 g/100 g) and saturated fat (approximately 19.8 g/100 g), making it one of the fattier beef cuts available. Protein content is moderate at 11.6 g/100 g, reflecting the high bone and fat proportion relative to lean muscle. Energy density is high at 492 kcal/100 g. Carbohydrate content is effectively negligible for this raw meat cut. Sodium is relatively low at 50 mg/100 g in the raw state, though cooking methods (braising with soy sauce, stock, or seasoning) can substantially increase sodium. Clinically, the very high saturated fat content is a concern for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. Ruminant trans fat (approximately 0.5 g/100 g from vaccenic acid and conjugated linoleic acid) is present in the raw fat. Oxtail is halal and gluten-free in its raw state. It is typically prepared by slow braising, stewing, or pressure cooking to tenderise the tough connective tissue.

Is raw oxtail good for you?
What nutrients are in raw oxtail?
How much saturated fat in oxtail?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Foodo Singapore database for the exact food item (cr_id F06030041, Oxtail raw). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 13325 (beef oxtail, raw) as the closest approved secondary source, since HPB does not report fatty acid fractions. Carbohydrate is reported as not available in the HPB source and is set to -1.
notes: carbohydrate_g is set to -1: the HPB Foodo source for Oxtail, raw (cr_id F06030041) does not report a carbohydrate value; this is consistent with a raw bone-in meat cut where carbohydrate is effectively absent but not explicitly confirmed as zero by the source. monounsaturated_fat_g (20.5), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.7), trans_fat_g (0.5) estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 13325 (beef oxtail, raw) as HPB does not report these fatty acid fractions. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for oxtail raw are not fully reported in the HPB Foodo database and were not available in a directly matching approved source entry at the time of processing. sugar_g is set to 0.0: naturally zero for a raw meat cut with no added ingredients. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero for a raw meat product. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; glycemic_index is set to the numeric value 0 as a sentinel to satisfy numeric field requirements, and gi_category is set to not_applicable to signal that GI testing is not meaningful for this food. Saturated fat value of ~19.8 g/100 g is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central NDB 13325 and is consistent with the input record health suitability annotations.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is the precise canonical English name 'Oxtail, raw'. No mandatory local-language names required as this is a generic global raw ingredient, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (492.0), protein_g (11.6), fat_g (47.1) sourced from HPB Foodo (exact match). carbohydrate_g is -1 with documented justification — HPB source does not report this value for the raw cut. nutrient_source fully populated with HPB as primary and USDA FoodData Central as secondary for fatty acid fractions.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (50.0), saturated_fat_g (19.8 estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 13325), sugar_g (0.0 confirmed zero for raw meat) are all present as numeric values and plausible.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is set to numeric value 0 (sentinel for not-applicable low-carbohydrate food), gi_category is 'not_applicable', glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0, gi_source is fully populated. The validator feedback from iteration 2 stated 'glycemic_index must be numeric'; this iteration corrects the null to the numeric sentinel 0 while gi_category remains not_applicable to preserve semantic accuracy.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided for beef oxtail (FOODON_03311349). FoodEx2 code A0CKL used as closest defensible parent node for raw cattle meat with match_type 'closest_parent' and gap note explaining the adaptation. SNOMED CT omitted with honest gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters (approximately 1,450 characters). Covers food identity, preparation methods, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrient profile.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern), Type 2 Diabetes (high concern, human_condition_name correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes'), Cardiovascular Disease (high concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid arthritis included as additional condition.
